label: Hungarian Parliament
sub: Power transition epicenter. New National Assembly inaugural session set for May 9, 2026.

label: Tisza Party HQ
sub: Magyar's transition base. Tisza Párt (TISZA — Tisztelet és Szabadság) formed February 2024.

label: Fidesz Party HQ
sub: Orbán retains party chairmanship here. Fidesz enters opposition for first time since 2010.

label: Constitutional Court
sub: Packed with Fidesz loyalists 2010–2013 after expansion from 11 to 15 members. Key institution for democratic reform.

label: MTVA State Media
sub: State broadcaster targeted for reform under Magyar government. KESMA media monopoly controls 80%+ of Hungarian market.

label: Sándor Palace (Presidential Office)
sub: Katalin Novák resigned here Feb 10, 2024 over child abuse pardon scandal. Tamás Sulyok serves as President from March 2024.

label: Central European University (former Budapest campus)
sub: CEU forced to relocate to Vienna by Lex CEU (Apr 2017). US-accredited programs moved by Dec 2019. Symbol of academic freedom erosion.

label: Kossuth Square — Parliament Protest Site
sub: Site of major anti-government protests. Tens of thousands gathered here on Jan 1, 2012 against new Fundamental Law and in Dec 2018 against Slave Law.

label: Heroes' Square — Magyar Rally Site
sub: Péter Magyar's annual March 15 rallies: 100,000+ in 2024, 150,000–200,000 in 2025. Largest post-communist opposition demonstrations in Hungarian history.

label: Kúria (Supreme Court)
sub: Hungary's highest court. Presidency given to Fidesz-aligned Zsolt Varga under Orbán. Independence questioned by Venice Commission and Freedom House.

label: National Bank of Hungary (MNB)
sub: MNB raised rates aggressively to combat 25.7% peak inflation (Jan 2023). HUF/EUR rate reached ~400 at worst; strengthened to ~365 post-election.

label: European Commission Representation
sub: EU Commission office in Budapest. Scene of tense diplomatic exchanges over frozen funds and rule-of-law procedures since 2022.

label: Paks Nuclear Power Plant
sub: Provides ~50% of Hungary's electricity. Paks II expansion (Rosatom, €12.5B, €10B Russian loan) began construction Feb 5, 2026. Security risk flagged by EU allies.

label: Felcsút — Puskás Akadémia Stadium
sub: Orbán's hometown (pop. 1,800). Site of €3.8B Puskás Akadémia stadium and youth football academy built with EU cohesion funds. Symbol of Fidesz cronyism.

label: Röszke Border Crossing — Serbia
sub: Site of Sep 16, 2015 clashes: Hungarian police used tear gas and water cannons on migrants. 4m razor-wire fence along 175km Serbia border built July–Sep 2015.

label: Záhony — Ukraine Border Crossing
sub: Main crossing point on Hungary-Ukraine border. Hungary exempted from sanctions on Druzhba pipeline oil imports. Strategic energy chokepoint in Orbán-EU tensions over Ukraine war.

label: Debrecen — EV Battery Megafactories
sub: Hungary's EV battery capital: CATL €7.3B, Samsung SDI €3.5B, BMW €2B factories. Cumulative battery FDI exceeds €20B. Major beneficiary of China Belt and Road investment.

label: Győr — Audi Engine Plant
sub: Audi Hungaria manufactures ~2M engines annually. Largest Audi facility globally. Hungary's export-oriented manufacturing base underpinned GDP growth 2013–2022 before German auto sector slowdown.

label: Kecskemét — Mercedes-Benz Plant
sub: Mercedes-Benz Hungary manufactures the A-Class and B-Class. Part of EU cohesion-fund-backed foreign direct investment that drove Hungary's pre-2022 economic growth model.

label: Hódmezővásárhely — Opposition By-Election Win
sub: February 2018 by-election: opposition independent Péter Márki-Zay defeated Fidesz in this traditionally safe seat. First signal that a united opposition could crack Fidesz in 2022.

label: Sopron — Austria Border Crossing
sub: Hungary's main western crossing into EU Schengen zone. Site of 1989 Pan-European Picnic that opened the Iron Curtain. Symbol of Hungary's EU integration now strained by Orbán-era drift.

label: Miskolc — Industrial Heartland
sub: Hungary's third-largest city. Fidesz stronghold in northeast. Opposition won mayoral race in 2019. Key bellwether region for Tisza's rural penetration in 2026 election.

label: Esztergom — Catholic Primacy See
sub: Seat of Cardinal Péter Erdő, Archbishop of Esztergom-Budapest. Fidesz cultivated close church-state relationship: confessional schools funded, Catholic church given major state assets since 2011.

label: Pécs — University City
sub: Hungary's fifth-largest city, home to University of Pécs. Fidesz stronghold in south Transdanubia. Budapest and Pécs represent Hungary's cultural-political divide on EU values questions.

label: Nyíregyháza — Eastern Gateway
sub: Hub city near Ukrainian border. NATO's eastern flank proximity made Hungary's Ukraine policy blocking especially sensitive. Druzhba oil pipeline passes through eastern Hungary near this region.
